definition: Yama, Niyama, Asana, Pranayama, Pratyahara, Dharana, Dhyana, Samadhi
Ethical restraints governing behavior toward others: non-violence (Ahimsa), truthfulness (Satya), non-stealing (Asteya), continence (Brahmacharya), non-possessiveness (Aparigraha)
Personal observances and self-disciplines: cleanliness (Saucha), contentment (Santosha), austerity (Tapas), self-study (Svadhyaya), surrender to the divine (Ishvara Pranidhana)
Physical postures; cultivating steadiness and ease in the body, preparing it for deeper practice
Breath control; regulation and extension of prana (life force) through breathing techniques
Withdrawal of the senses; drawing awareness inward and away from external stimuli
Concentration; sustained, focused attention on a single point or object
Meditation; unbroken, continuous flow of concentration leading toward absorption
Blissful absorption; complete union with the object of meditation; enlightenment
Nasagrai, Bhrumadhya, Nabi Chakra, Hastagrai, Padhayoragrai, Parsva, Angusta Ma Dyai, Urdhva
Drishti: gaze fixed at the tip of the nose
Drishti: gaze fixed at the third eye center, between the eyebrows (Ajna Chakra)
Drishti: gaze fixed at the navel center
Drishti: gaze fixed at the hand or fingertips
Drishti: gaze fixed at the tips of the toes or feet
Drishti: gaze fixed far to the side (right or left)
Drishti: gaze fixed at the thumbs
Drishti: gaze fixed upward toward the sky

term: Name the 4 Ligaments of the Knee
definition: Anterior Cruciate Ligament (ACL), Posterior Cruciate Ligament (PCL), Lateral Collateral Ligament (LCL), Medial Collateral Ligament (MCL)
Flexion, Extension, Adduction, Abduction, Pronation, Supination, Rotation, Dorsiflexion, Plantar Flexion
Decreasing the angle between two body parts by bending a joint (e.g., bending the knee or elbow)
Increasing the angle between two body parts by straightening a joint (e.g., straightening the knee or elbow)
Movement of a limb toward the midline of the body
Movement of a limb away from the midline of the body
Rotation of the forearm so the palm faces downward; also refers to inward rolling of the foot/ankle
Rotation of the forearm so the palm faces upward; also refers to outward rolling of the foot/ankle
Movement of a body part around its own longitudinal axis
Flexion of the foot upward toward the shin, decreasing the angle at the ankle joint
Extension of the foot downward away from the shin (pointing the toes), increasing the angle at the ankle joint

Stretches hamstrings, groins, hips, IT band, and spine; strengthens thighs, knees, and ankles; opens chest and shoulders; stimulates abdominal organs; improves digestion; relieves back pain and sciatica; builds balance and stability; reduces stress and anxiety

term: 3–4 Prep Exercises for Downward Dog — and why
definition: 1. Cat-Cow: warms the spine, mobilizes wrists and shoulders; 2. Plank Pose: builds the shoulder stability and core strength needed to hold DD; 3. Puppy Pose (Uttana Shishosana): opens upper back and stretches lats, mimicking DD arm extension; 4. Standing Forward Fold (Uttanasana): lengthens hamstrings and calves, easing the posterior chain tension that prevents heels from grounding

term: Name the 9 Obstacles to Yoga Sadhana (Antarayas)
definition: Vyadhi, Styana, Samshaya, Pramada, Alasya, Avirati, Bhranti Darshana, Alabdha Bhumikatva, Anavasthitatva
Antaraya: physical disease or ailment (e.g., chronic pain) that disrupts practice
Antaraya: mental dullness or lack of interest; apathy toward practice
Antaraya: doubt; uncertainty about the path or one's own capability
Antaraya: carelessness or procrastination; neglecting consistent practice
Antaraya: physical laziness; heaviness of body that resists effort
Antaraya: craving of the senses; inability to detach from sensory pleasures
Antaraya: false perception or misconception; deluded understanding of the practice or self
Antaraya: failure to attain the ground of higher practice; unable to reach advanced stages
Antaraya: inability to maintain or stabilize attainments; falling back from achieved states
Asana, Kumbhaka, Mudra, Bandha, Dhyana, Mantra
Stable, comfortable posture that allows free breath movement and sustained concentration
Breath retention; holding the breath after inhalation (antara kumbhaka) or after exhalation (bahya kumbhaka)
Gestures or "mental attitudes" that redirect prana; 108 mudras are available
Energy locks that contain and direct prana (e.g., Mula Bandha, Uddiyana Bandha, Jalandhara Bandha)
Awareness; directing focused attention to a specific chakra or internal focal point during practice
Mental chant used to tame and focus the mind during pranayama practice
Space (Akasha), Air (Vayu), Fire (Agni), Water (Jala), Earth (Prithvi)

term: Name the 12 Steps of Hatha Yoga Surya Namaskar (Bihar School)
definition: Pranamasana, Hasta Uttanasana, Padhastasana, Ashwa Sanchalanasana, Parvatasana, Ashtanga Namaskara, Bhujangasana, Parvatasana, Ashwa Sanchalanasana, Padhastasana, Hasta Uttanasana, Pranamasana
Surya Namaskar — Step 1 | Exhale; stand with hands in prayer position at the heart
Surya Namaskar — Step 2 | Inhale; raise arms overhead with a slight backbend
Surya Namaskar — Step 3 | Exhale; fold forward, hands touching the floor beside the feet
Surya Namaskar — Step 4 | Inhale; step the left leg back into a low lunge, gaze forward
Surya Namaskar — Step 5 | Exhale; step the right leg back and raise the hips into Downward Dog (Mountain Pose)
Surya Namaskar — Step 6 | Same breath (no new inhale); lower knees, chest, and chin to the ground — eight-limbed salutation
Surya Namaskar — Step 7 | Inhale; slide forward and lift the chest into Cobra Pose
Surya Namaskar — Step 8 | Exhale; press back into Downward Dog (Mountain Pose)
Surya Namaskar — Step 9 | Inhale; step the left leg forward into a low lunge
Surya Namaskar — Step 10 | Exhale; step the right leg forward into a standing forward fold
Surya Namaskar — Step 11 | Inhale; sweep arms up overhead with a slight backbend
Surya Namaskar — Step 12 | Exhale; return hands to prayer position at the heart (repeat with opposite legs)
Sthiran Sukham Asanam — a posture that is stable (physically and emotionally) and comfortable, with correct alignment (Yoga Sutras II.46)
Jnana Yoga, Bhakti Yoga, Karma Yoga, Raja Yoga
Path of knowledge and wisdom; liberation through study, self-inquiry, and discernment between the real and unreal
Path of devotion; liberation through love, surrender, and worship of the divine
Path of selfless action; liberation through service performed without attachment to results
The royal path; liberation through meditation and the 8 limbs of Patanjali's Ashtanga system

term: Name the 6 Astika Schools (Orthodox Hindu Philosophy)
definition: Nyaya, Vaisheshika, Samkhya, Yoga, Mimamsa (Purva Mimamsa), Vedanta (Uttara Mimamsa)
Orthodox school of logic and epistemology; establishes the four valid means of knowledge (Pramanas)
Orthodox school of atomism; categorizes all reality into substances, qualities, and actions
Orthodox dualistic school; enumerates 25 tattvas arising from Purusha (pure consciousness) and Prakriti (matter)
Orthodox school of Patanjali; practical 8-limbed path to liberate Purusha from Prakriti through discipline and meditation
Orthodox school of Vedic ritual interpretation; emphasizes correct performance of dharma and sacrificial rites
Orthodox school exploring the nature of Brahman; encompasses Advaita (non-dualism), Dvaita (dualism), and Vishishtadvaita among others
Buddhism, Jainism, Charvaka
Heterodox school founded by Siddhartha Gautama; rejects Vedic authority; teaches the Four Noble Truths, Eightfold Path, impermanence, and no-self (anatta)
Heterodox school associated with Mahavira; rejects Vedic authority; teaches ahimsa, anekantavada (non-absolutism), and liberation through non-attachment
Heterodox materialist school; rejects the Vedas and all metaphysics; holds that only direct perception (pratyaksha) is valid knowledge
Vata, Pitta, Kapha
Composed of air (Vayu) and space (Akasha); governs movement, creativity, and the nervous system; imbalance manifests as anxiety, dryness, and irregular digestion
Composed of fire (Agni) and water (Jala); governs transformation, digestion, and metabolism; imbalance manifests as anger, inflammation, and overheating
Composed of earth (Prithvi) and water (Jala); governs stability, lubrication, and structure; imbalance manifests as lethargy, congestion, and weight gain
Ahimsa, Satya, Asteya, Brahmacharya, Aparigraha
Non-violence; refraining from harm in thought, word, and deed toward all living beings
Truthfulness; alignment of thought, speech, and action with truth
Non-stealing; not taking what has not been freely given, including time, ideas, or energy
Right use of energy or continence; traditionally celibacy; broadly the conservation and redirection of vital energy away from sensory indulgence
Non-possessiveness; not hoarding; taking only what is necessary and releasing attachment to outcomes
Dhauti, Basti, Neti, Nauli, Trataka, Kapalabhati
Internal cleansing; 13 types including voluntary vomiting (Kunjal Kriya) and cloth swallowing (Vastra Dhauti); purifies the digestive tract
Yogic enema; cleansing of the colon using water; removes waste and balances Apana Vayu
Nasal passage cleansing; Jala Neti uses warm saline water; Sutra Neti uses a waxed thread; clears sinuses and supports pranayama
Abdominal massage through isolation and churning of the rectus abdominis; massages internal organs and stimulates digestive fire (Agni)
One-pointed gazing, typically at a candle flame without blinking; purifies and strengthens the eyes; develops concentration (Dharana)
Skull-shining breath; forceful rapid exhalations with passive inhalations; cleanses the respiratory tract and energizes the mind
The inner instrument; the fourfold internal faculty through which consciousness operates: Mana, Buddhi, Citta, Ahamkara

term: Name the 4 Components of Anta Karna (Antahkarana)
definition: Mana, Buddhi, Citta, Ahamkara
The indecisive mind; receives and processes sensory input; generates doubts and mental fluctuations (vrittis)
The intellect; the decisive faculty; discerns, determines, and provides clarity and wisdom
Memory and the subconscious; the storehouse of impressions (samskaras) and past experiences
The ego; the "I"-maker; creates the sense of individual identity, ownership, and doership
Yoga citta vritti nirodha — yoga is the cessation of fluctuations of the mind
Traditional and foundational branch of yoga combining asanas, pranayama, and meditation to balance mind and body
A connected series of asanas with synchronized breath that functions as moving meditation; benefits include improved focus and reduced anxiety, depression, stress responses, and insomnia
